blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Perinatal Arrhythmias?
Arrhythmias พบใน 1-2% ของทุก pregnancy; hemodynamic compromise + hydrops ใน 10% ของ fetal arrhythmias. PAC พบใน 25-50% ของ newborns — usually benign; แต่ 0.5-1% พัฒนาไปเป็น tachycardia. SVT (ORT 70%, flutter 30%) = most common fetal tachyarrhythmia; ≥220 bpm → hydrops risk. CCAVB: 1/15,000-20,000 births; 40% immune-mediated (anti-SSA/Ro); dexamethasone สำหรับ 2nd degree. Incessant SVT → tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy → cardiovascular collapse ถ้า miss diagnosis.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย cardiac conduction system: SA node, AV node, His-Purkinje
แยก 3 mechanisms of arrhythmia: enhanced automaticity, reentry, triggered automaticity
แยก ectopic beats (PAC, PVC): benign vs pathologic, no treatment required
รู้ ORT (most common SVT): mechanism, rate 240-300 bpm, adenosine treatment, propranolol chronic
แยก SVT types: ORT vs PJRT vs AET vs JET vs atrial flutter — by VA relationship + rate + onset
รู้ CCAVB: etiology (anti-SSA/Ro), escape rate, pacemaker indications (HR <55 bpm, wide QRS, CHD)
รู้ Long QT syndrome: 2:1 functional block, TdP, congenital forms
รู้ Wolff-Parkinson-White: delta wave, contraindication digoxin
รู้ fetal arrhythmia management: digoxin/sotalol/flecainide for fetal SVT; sotalol first-line for fetal flutter
รู้ fetal AV block: anti-SSA/Ro → dexamethasone; terbutaline for HR <55 bpm
1. Conduction System of the Human Heart
1.1 SA Node
Located between vena cava and right atrium (predominantly near SVC-RA junction)
Histologically distinct from atrial myocardium; automatic action potential behavior
Functional testing: nodal tissue spans lateral wall RA from SVC to IVC
1.2 AV Node
Posterior right atrium, interatrial septum, anterior to tricuspid valve
Compact AV node: only conduction channel into bundle of His in normal hearts
Slows conduction velocity → ensures ventricular activation after atrial activation
1.3 His-Purkinje System
Bundle of His → right bundle branch + left bundle (anterior + posterior fascicles)
Insulated from ventricular myocardium until terminal branches
Purkinje fibers → coordinated, synchronized ventricular contraction
2. Mechanisms of Arrhythmia
2.1 Enhanced Automaticity
Abnormal cluster of cells with ↑automaticity > sinus node
Gradual onset ("warm-up") and offset ("cool-down"); beat-to-beat variability
Influenced by autonomic tone, drugs: caffeine, dopamine, epinephrine
Examples: AET, JET, AIVR
Can be overdrive-suppressed by faster rhythm
2.2 Reentry
Requires: circuit with antegrade + retrograde limbs; differential conduction (one limb slows/blocks); circuit can support repetitive fixed rate
Abrupt onset + termination; fixed rate; no warm-up
Examples: ORT (accessory pathway), PJRT, atrial flutter (macro-reentry around tricuspid valve)
2.3 Triggered Automaticity
Enhanced by: acidosis, mechanical stimulation, myocardial injury/inflammation
Drugs: dopamine, epinephrine, caffeine → ↑automaticity in ectopic foci
3. Ectopic Beats
3.1 Premature Atrial Complexes (PAC)
PAC: 25-50% of normal newborns; resolves within first few months of life
On ECG: early P wave with different axis/morphology from sinus P wave
Conducted normally (narrow QRS), aberrantly conducted (wide QRS = Ashman phenomenon), or blocked (PAC finds AV node refractory → no QRS → "pause")
Blocked atrial bigeminy → halves effective heart rate → looks like bradycardia
Fetal PAC: 0.5-1% risk of progression to tachycardia; 2% associated with LQTS/flutter/AV block → fetal echo if frequent
Treatment: NONE required for isolated PAC
3.2 Premature Ventricular Complexes (PVC)
Wide QRS, T-wave axis different from normal QRS-T, no preceding P wave
Isolated PVC in structurally normal heart: benign, no treatment
AIVR (accelerated idioventricular rhythm): "slow VT" — monomorphic, rate slightly > sinus — catecholamine states, electrolyte disturbance → benign
4. Tachyarrhythmias
Differential diagnosis by VA relationship:
4.1 Orthodromic Reciprocating Tachycardia (ORT) — MOST COMMON
Most common SVT in fetus and neonate (60-70% of all SVT)
Mechanism: reentry using normal AV node/His-Purkinje as antegrade + accessory pathway as retrograde
Rate: fixed 240-300 bpm; abrupt onset/termination; short RP interval
Concealed pathway (retrograde only) vs manifest pathway (WPW — preexcitation: short PR, delta wave, wide QRS)
WPW: delta wave visible in sinus rhythm; tachycardia = narrow QRS ORT; CONTRAINDICATION: digoxin (↑accessory pathway conduction)
Acute: vagal maneuvers (knee-chest, diving reflex/ice to face); adenosine 0.05-0.1 mg/kg rapid IV bolus (escalate as needed)
Chronic: propranolol first-line; digoxin (avoid if WPW); second-line: sotalol, flecainide, amiodarone
4.2 Permanent Form of Junctional Reciprocating Tachycardia (PJRT)
Incessant SVT (reinitiated immediately after termination)
Rate: 180-200 bpm (near upper limit of normal) → often MISSED diagnosis
Long RP interval (slow retrograde conduction via posterior septal/tricuspid accessory pathway)
Hallmark: strongly negative retrograde P waves in leads II, III, aVF
Often presents with tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy (missed for weeks/months)
Treatment: challenging (incessant); multiple agents often needed; sotalol, amiodarone, flecainide
4.3 Atrial Ectopic Tachycardia (AET)
Less common; automatic (NOT reentry); focus in atrium outside SA node
Abnormal P wave axis on ECG; paroxysmal or incessant; variable rate (slightly > sinus to >300 bpm)
Warm-up/cool-down behavior; intermittent AV block (2:1, Wenckebach) can be seen (diagnostic feature!)
Adenosine: only transiently blocks ventricular conduction; atrial activity continues → diagnostic
DC cardioversion: reinitiation likely
Treatment: catecholamine-sensitive → β-blockade; flecainide; amiodarone for incessant
4.4 Junctional Ectopic Tachycardia (JET)
Enhanced automaticity of AV junction; warm-up/cool-down behavior
Acquired: post-cardiac surgery (up to 20%) of certain operations — self-limited
Congenital JET: incessant → tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy
Rate: 170-210 bpm; retrograde atrial activation (may be dissociated)
Treatment: amiodarone (most effective for congenital JET); hypothermia + procainamide for postoperative JET
4.5 Neonatal Atrial Flutter
Macro-reentry around tricuspid valve; atrial rate ≥300 bpm; "sawtooth" in inferior leads (II, III, aVF)
AV conduction: variable (1:1 — neonates have faster AV conduction! vs 2:1, 3:1 in older)
1:1 conduction → very rapid ventricular rate → heart failure
Adenosine: diagnostic (reveals flutter waves while blocking AV node); doesn't terminate flutter
Treatment: DC cardioversion (highly effective, low recurrence); medical rate control while preparing
Fetal flutter: often 2:1; sotalol first-line; postnatal sync cardioversion recommended after delivery
Co-occurrence with ORT in fetus: 12-33%
4.6 Ventricular Tachycardia
AIVR: benign slow VT (near sinus rate); no treatment usually
Monomorphic VT in infancy: usually benign; medical management
Polymorphic VT / Torsades de Pointes (TdP): life-threatening; twisting QRS morphology
TdP in neonate: almost always LQTS (see below); or electrolyte disturbance, drug-induced
5. Bradyarrhythmias
5.1 Sinus Bradycardia
Normal term neonate: sinus rate 90 bpm during sleep first 24-48 hr — normal
Causes: vagal stimulation (NGT, OGT, ETT, GER), therapeutic hypothermia, medications (β-blockers)
5.2 Blocked PAC
PAC on T wave → refractory AV node/Purkinje → no QRS; pause before next sinus beat
Blocked atrial bigeminy → functional 2:1 ratio → apparent bradycardia
Careful ECG reading: find hidden P wave in T wave → diagnosis
Fetal blocked PAC: can mimic 2nd degree AV block on M-mode echo; no treatment; 10% fetal SVT risk
5.3 Long QT Syndrome (LQTS)
Severe QT prolongation → 2:1 functional AV block (ventricles refractory when next P wave arrives)
QTc >450 ms in males, >460 ms in females; severe: >500 ms
TdP → potentially lethal; in neonate: LQTS until proven otherwise
Treatment: β-blockade (propranolol); magnesium; avoid QT-prolonging drugs; pacemaker if recurrent TdP/arrest
Congenital forms: LQT1 (KCNQ1), LQT2 (KCNH2), LQT3 (SCN5A) most common
5.4 Congenital Complete AV Block (CCAVB)
Incidence: 1/15,000-20,000 births
Immune-mediated (40%): anti-SSA/Ro + anti-SSB/La antibodies → fibrosis of AV nodal structures; risk 2-3% if mother antibody-positive; recurrence 12-17% after one affected child
Structural CHD (50%): L-TGA (congenitally corrected TGA) most common; superficial His bundle
Idiopathic (10%)
Pacemaker indications (PACES 2021):

rows: Mean ventricular rate ≤50 bpm in neonate
CCAVB + LV dilation (z-score ≥3) + MR or systolic dysfunction
Asymptomatic adolescent with acceptable rate + narrow QRS + normal function

blocks.text: ECG features: P wave rate ~150 bpm; completely dissociated junctional escape rate 60-80 bpm; narrow QRS usually (His intact); wide QRS escape = ventricular origin → higher risk

Postnatal management: isoproterenol, epinephrine, dobutamine for ↑HR; temporary transvenous pacing (technically difficult due to vessel size in neonate); emergent temporary pacing possible via umbilical vein
6. Management of Neonatal Tachyarrhythmias

rows: 0.05-0.1 mg/kg rapid IV bolus; escalate; not effective for AET/flutter (but diagnostic)
Monitor glucose (hypoglycemia in preterm); contraindicated in CHF
Contraindicated in WPW (↑accessory pathway conduction); not effective for AET
QT + QRS monitoring; bound by milk protein → ↑levels with breast milk feeds

blocks.text: 7. Fetal Arrhythmias
7.1 Assessment

Fetal echocardiography: mechanical surrogate for ECG; measure atrial + ventricular systoles + relationship (M-mode across atrial + ventricular walls; spectral Doppler of mitral inflow + aortic outflow)
"Mechanical PR interval" = time from mitral inflow to aortic outflow
VA time (ventriculoatrial): from aortic outflow to next mitral inflow; short VA → ORT; long VA → AET/PJRT
Fetal ECG or magnetocardiography: LQTS diagnosis (QTc measurement)
7.2 Fetal SVT Management

rows: Sotalol or flecainide if hydrops or digoxin failure

blocks.text: Digoxin in hydrops: transplacental transfer ↓ to 25%; consider direct fetal injection if severely hydropic + <37 wk
Goal: NOT 100% sinus rhythm → sufficient sinus rhythm to resolve hydrops + ventricular dysfunction
Emergency delivery: refractory hydropic fetus; consider if near-term + delivery low-risk
7.3 Fetal Bradycardia + AV Block
Anti-SSA/Ro → fibrosis of AV nodal structures; risk highest between 16-26 wk gestation
Screening: echo q2 weeks at 16-26 wk for all fetuses with maternal anti-SSA/SSB antibodies; fetal HR monitoring twice daily (home monitoring 16-26 wk → detect evolving 2nd degree block quickly)
Dexamethasone 4-8 mg/day: reduces inflammation; may reverse/stabilize 2nd degree block; improve hydrops; CONTROVERSIAL for complete block; risks: IUGR, oligohydramnios, CNS effects
Hydroxychloroquine before 10 wk: ↓risk anti-SSA-mediated cardiac disease in high-risk pregnancies (Izmirly 2012)
Terbutaline: β-sympathomimetic; ↑fetal HR if <55 bpm; maternal tachycardia side effect (100-120 bpm)
Fetal pacing: experimental; last resort
8. NICU Practical
Irregular fetal rhythm found on Doppler → fetal echo to differentiate: PAC (benign) vs AV block vs tachycardia
Neonate with sudden HR >220 bpm + poor feeding/pallor → SVT until proven otherwise; ECG immediately
Adenosine administration: use large IV closest to heart (antecubital or umbilical); flush rapidly immediately after; have resuscitation ready; repeat escalating doses
Chronic SVT in infant: propranolol PO (1-2 mg/kg/day divided q6-8h) after excluding WPW on ECG; monitor glucose
WPW on ECG (short PR + delta wave): avoid digoxin; use propranolol or flecainide
PJRT: suspect if infant with CHF + "mild tachycardia" (180-200 bpm) → cardiac echo + ECG carefully
CCAVB in neonate: ECG shows complete AV dissociation; echo for CHD evaluation; mean rate <55 bpm or wide QRS → pacemaker urgently
Fetal complete heart block + hydrops: poor prognosis; consider dexamethasone for maternal antibody-mediated; terbutaline if HR <55 bpm; cardiac cath + pacemaker postnatally
LQTS suspect: QTc >470 ms in first week of life → cardiology referral; avoid QT-prolonging drugs; β-blockade (propranolol)
9. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — PAC ≠ Emergency, But Blocked Bigeminy Mimics Bradycardia
PAC พบ 25-50% ทารกแรกเกิด; blocked atrial bigeminy → apparent HR drop → ดูเหมือน bradycardia; ECG carefully: ectopic P wave hidden in T wave = blocked PAC (ไม่ใช่ AV block); no treatment needed; reassure parents; fetal PAC: fetal echo ถ้า frequent (>1 in every 3-5 beats)

PEARL 2 — "Mildly Tachycardic" Infant + CHF = PJRT Until Proven Otherwise
PJRT rate 180-200 bpm — ดูเหมือน sinus tachycardia; ถ้า PJRT นาน → dilated cardiomyopathy; characteristic: deeply negative P waves in leads II, III, aVF; incessant (immediately recurs after cardioversion); key: ถ้าทารกมี CHF + HR ≥170 bpm → ทำ 12-lead ECG; check VA relationship + P wave morphology ใน inferior leads

PEARL 3 — Digoxin + WPW = Dangerous
Digoxin ↑vagal tone → ↑AV node refractoriness แต่ accidentally ↓accessory pathway refractoriness → ↑accessory pathway conduction; ถ้ามี atrial fibrillation → rapid ventricular conduction via accessory pathway → VF; ก่อนให้ digoxin สำหรับ SVT → ตรวจ ECG ให้ครบ: ตรวจ WPW (delta wave, short PR) → ถ้าเจอ WPW → ใช้ propranolol แทน

PEARL 4 — Fetal CCAVB: Monitor Every 2 Weeks During 16-26 wk
Anti-SSA/Ro → AV node fibrosis risk 2-3%; ถ้า mother positive → fetal echo q2 wk ที่ 16-26 wk (highest risk period); home fetal HR monitoring twice daily; ถ้าเจอ 2nd degree block → ADMIT + dexamethasone immediately (window period to prevent progression to 3rd degree); complete block detected เองไม่สามารถ reverse → aim for secondary prevention

PEARL 5 — Adenosine: ฉีดเร็วที่สุด ใกล้หัวใจที่สุด
Adenosine half-life <10 sec; technique critical: (1) IV ใกล้หัวใจที่สุด (antecubital > hand); (2) Draw adenosine + NS flush in connected syringes; (3) Push adenosine rapidly then flush immediately; (4) หน้า monitor continuous ECG; (5) ถ้า flutter → adenosine จะ block AV node → reveal sawtooth flutter waves (diagnostic) แต่ไม่ terminate flutter; SVT/ORT → likely terminate; ถ้าไม่ terminate → escalate dose
10. Evidence Update

Donofrio MT et al. Circulation. 2014;129:2183 (AHA Scientific Statement): Diagnosis and treatment of fetal cardiac disease. Comprehensive guidelines fetal arrhythmia management: PAC, SVT, flutter, VT, AV block — definitive reference.

Jaeggi ET et al. Circulation. 2011;124:1747: Comparison of transplacental treatment fetal SVT with digoxin, flecainide, sotalol: all effective as monotherapy; sotalol/flecainide superior in hydropic fetus.
Shah A et al. Am J Cardiol. 2012;109:1614: Sotalol as first-line therapy for fetal SVT including flutter — effective conversion 50-80%.

Jaeggi ET et al. Circulation. 2004;110:1542: Transplacental treatment CCAVB without CHD with betamethasone: improved outcome (↓cardiac pacing need, ↑ventricular rate) — controversial but important study.

Shah MJ, Silka MJ et al. Heart Rhythm. 2021;18:1925 (PACES 2021): Expert consensus indications and management cardiovascular implantable electronic devices in pediatric patients — current pacemaker indications table for CCAVB.
Kang KT et al. Heart Rhythm. 2014;11:1426: PJRT multicenter experience — often presents with cardiomyopathy; difficult to control; highlights importance of early recognition.
Casey FA et al. Am Heart J. 1997;133:302: Neonatal atrial flutter — significant early morbidity; DC cardioversion highly effective; low recurrence rate.
Izmirly PM et al. Circulation. 2012;126:76: Maternal hydroxychloroquine associated with ↓risk recurrent anti-SSA/Ro antibody cardiac manifestations in neonatal lupus — used in high-risk pregnancies.

Sonesson SE et al. Arthritis Rheum. 2004;50:1253: Signs of 1st degree heart block in 1/3 of fetuses with maternal anti-SSA/Ro 52-kd antibodies; Ro-52 specific antibody: close association with immune-mediated CCAVB.

Moffett BS et al. Cardiol Young. 2015;25:1080: Digoxin vs propranolol for infant SVT (large national database). Propranolol more successful as monotherapy; comparable overall success; growing use of propranolol as first-line.

Cuneo BF et al. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2018;72:1940: Home fetal heart monitoring during anti-Ro pregnancies 16-26 wk. Detects evolving 2nd degree AV block quickly → allows immediate admission + treatment.

Gopinathannair R et al. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2015;66:1714: Arrhythmia-induced cardiomyopathies — mechanisms, recognition, management. JET, AET, PJRT: incessant tachycardia → dilated cardiomyopathy → reversible with arrhythmia control.
11. Self-Check Questions พร้อมเฉลย
Q: ทารกแรกเกิดหัวใจเต้น 260 bpm แบบ abrupt onset — ควร approach อย่างไร?

ตอบ: SVT most likely (ORT most common, 240-300 bpm, fixed rate, abrupt onset/termination); ทำ ECG 12-lead ทันที; assess hemodynamic stability; ถ้า stable: vagal maneuvers (knee-chest or ice to face for diving reflex); ถ้าไม่ตอบ: adenosine 0.05 mg/kg rapid IV bolus nearest IV to heart (escalate to 0.1-0.2 mg/kg if needed); ถ้า unstable/severe CHF: DC cardioversion; หลัง terminate: check ECG in sinus rhythm for delta wave (WPW); chronic: propranolol if no WPW, avoid digoxin if WPW
Q: PJRT ต่างจาก ORT อย่างไร? และทำไมถึง miss diagnosis บ่อย?

ตอบ: ORT: rate 240-300 bpm, abrupt onset/termination, short VA interval; PJRT: rate 180-200 bpm (≈ upper sinus tachycardia!), incessant (immediately recurs), long VA interval, strongly NEGATIVE P waves in leads II/III/aVF; PJRT missed because: (1) rate not dramatically elevated, (2) infant shows symptoms gradually (not acute), (3) by time diagnosed: dilated cardiomyopathy; key distinguishing feature: negative P waves in inferior leads + incessant pattern
Q: Wolff-Parkinson-White — ECG features และทำไมต้อง avoid digoxin?

ตอบ: WPW ECG: (1) Short PR interval (<80-100 ms in neonate); (2) Delta wave (slurred initial QRS upstroke) from preexcitation; (3) Wide QRS; Tachycardia: narrow QRS ORT (retrograde via AP); avoid digoxin: digoxin ↑AV node refractoriness (beneficial for ORT) BUT also ↓accessory pathway refractoriness → if AF develops → rapid ventricular conduction via AP → VF → death; use propranolol (β-blocker) or flecainide instead
Q: CCAVB ใน neonate — เมื่อไหร่ต้อง pacemaker?

ตอบ: Absolute indications (PACES 2021): (1) Symptomatic bradycardia; (2) Wide QRS escape rhythm; (3) Complex ventricular ectopy or VD; (4) Mean ventricular rate ≤50 bpm; (5) CCAVB + CHD + rate <60-70 bpm; Reasonable: mean rate ≤50 bpm beyond year 1 + prolonged pauses; CCAVB + LV dilation z≥3 + MR/systolic dysfunction; Note: junctional escape narrow QRS 60-80 bpm can be adequate for some time; wide QRS escape = ventricular origin = higher risk → more urgent pacemaker
Q: Atrial flutter ใน neonate — treat อย่างไร?

ตอบ: DC cardioversion = treatment of choice (highly effective, low recurrence); medical rate control while preparing if needed (digoxin, sotalol); neonatal AV node conducts rapidly → 1:1 AV conduction possible → very rapid ventricular rate; adenosine: diagnostic (reveals flutter waves by blocking AV node) but does NOT terminate flutter; postnatal recurrence: rare; fetal flutter: sotalol first-line; procainamide: CONTRAINDICATED in fetal flutter
Q: Fetal SVT (ORT) — management algorithm?

ตอบ: Sustained SVT ≥200 bpm + no hydrops → transplacental antiarrhythmic therapy if GA <37 wk (deliver if ≥37 wk); First-line: digoxin OR sotalol OR flecainide as monotherapy — no RCT showing one superior; hydrops: transplacental transfer ↓ (digoxin → 25%) → flecainide/sotalol preferred; goal: sufficient sinus rhythm to resolve hydrops (not 100% conversion); direct fetal injection (IM or intracordal) only for severely hydropic + too early to deliver; amiodarone: third-line (significant maternal/fetal toxicity)
Q: Immune-mediated CCAVB — maternal screening + prevention?

ตอบ: Antibody screening: anti-SSA/Ro and anti-SSB/La; risk: 2-3% for first affected child; 12-17% recurrence after one affected child; peak risk: 16-26 wk; Fetal monitoring: echo q2 wk 16-26 wk; home fetal HR monitoring twice daily; Treatment 2nd degree (reversible): dexamethasone 4-8 mg/day → reduces inflammation, may reverse to 1st degree or stabilize; Complete heart block: dexamethasone usually ineffective for reversal; Prevention: hydroxychloroquine <10 wk (↓recurrence in high-risk); Postnatally: pacemaker if rate <55 bpm or CHD + rate <70 bpm
Q: Long QT syndrome in neonate — how to diagnose and manage?

ตอบ: Suspect: QTc >470 ms in first week (normal QTc <440 ms; corrected by Bazett: QTc = QT/√RR); 2:1 functional AV block if severe QT prolongation; TdP (polymorphic VT): ECG shows twisting QRS amplitude/axis; Management: β-blockade (propranolol 2-4 mg/kg/day); correct electrolytes (Mg2+, K+, Ca2+); avoid QT-prolonging drugs (erythromycin, metoclopramide, fluconazole, ondansetron); pacemaker if recurrent TdP/arrest; ICD in severe forms; genetic testing: LQT1 (KCNQ1), LQT2 (KCNH2), LQT3 (SCN5A) — each has different triggers and treatment nuances
Day 50 Preview

Avery Chapter 50 — Congenital Heart Disease (González Estévez & Ansah)
พรุ่งนี้: Congenital Heart Disease — บทที่ใหญ่ที่สุดของสัปดาห์นี้; ครอบคลุม fetal-to-postnatal transition, pulse oximetry screening, ductus-dependent lesions (TGA → BAS + arterial switch, TOF, HLHS Norwood/Sano/Hybrid, coarctation, IAA), genetics (22q11, trisomy 21, Turner), และ CHF management principles. Clinical pearl: ทารกแรกเกิดที่ทำท่าดีอยู่แล้วแย่ลงวันที่ 2-3 → ductal-dependent lesion จนกว่าจะพิสูจน์ไม่ใช่ → PGE1 ทันที!
